sections.gist: Introduction: maiden name, origin in Yugoslavia, and initial journey description
Description of the peasant home, attached barn, farm animals, and meat preparation
Heating the home, mother's life as a widow, and family composition
Walking to school, the train track incident, and bartering with neighbors
World War I memories: hosting refugees and washing clothes at the river
Languages spoken, political transition to Yugoslavia, barter system, and river crossing
Learning about the trip to America, obtaining papers, brother's death, departure, and school back home
Train journey to Trieste and the ship voyage to America
Arrival in New York, train trip to Utah, and starting school in Sunnyside
Mother's adjustment in Utah, the Depression, and the move to Colorado
Marriage, work at the packing house, daughters, and citizenship
Concluding remarks and end of interview

sentences.text: PHILLIPS:	This Andrew Phillips.
It's Monday the 28th of August, 1989.
I'm with Mary Grande, spelled G-R-A-N-D-E.  This is Interview Number 414 [DP-40] of the Ellis Island Oral History Project.
Commencing this interview at a little bit before 12:30.
Mrs. Grande, could you start by telling me what your name is and where you immigrated from.
GRANDE:	What my maiden name was?
PHILLIPS:	Uh, let me just get the microphone set up.
That's fine.
That's fine.
GRANDE:	My maiden name was, uh, Marija Yankovik.
And we came, my mother and I came from Kandijaright over Novo Mesto, which is in Yugoslavia now.
Originally it was Austria/Hungary.
And we came here, we left there August 12, 1920 and we stayed a few days in Trieste, Italy.
PHILLIPS:	Can you spell that for me, please?
GRANDE:	T-R-I-E-S-T-E, Italy.
And then we got on the ship.
I believe the name of it was America.
And it was right after World War One, so they stopped it in a lot of places.
In London, and I suppose Paris, and all over.
But part of our clothes was stolen in Trieste, so a fellow that my mother knew, he went in, I believe, in London, to buy some clothes for us.
He could speak American.
He came back from the States and who he bought back I don't remember.
But, uh, he bought some clothes so we had something to change in to, to wear.
PHILLIPS:	Before you tell us about travelling from your home country to the United States, could you tell us a little bit about what your home was like.
For instance, start by telling us what your parents did for a living.
GRANDE:	Well, my father was here in the States since 1911.
In 		fact, he was, during World War one here in the United States, and my mother was a farmer, and she raised some cattle and bartered with them.
PHILLIPS:	Tell me what your home was like.
What did it look like?
GRANDE:	It was an average peasant home.
It was three rooms, living room and kitchen in the middle and a small room on the side.
And then facing what would be, I guess, the east side was half, was a barn where the cattle were.
PHILLIPS:	So the cattle were actually, they were part of the 		house.
The barn was part of the house.
GRANDE:	Yes.
PHILLIPS:	Explain that to us, what was that like.

GRANDE:	Well, it was all one building and there were, later on they made that as all one room, but at that time we had a half a room was like for the barn, for the cows, or when my brother had horses it was there.
And then there was a manure pile just to the side of it.
And the majority of people had it similar like that.
PHILLIPS:	Why were the houses and the barns constructed together 		like that?
GRANDE:	It's been historical, as far as I know.
I don't know any different.
They don't do it now any more like that, but they used to.
PHILLIPS:	And what was it like for a little girl to be living so close to the animals?

Did you enjoy that, or were you--

	GRANDE:	Oh, yes.
I enjoyed it because they were part of my life.
In fact, I had a pet cow that I loved very much.
And then the pigs were, the pig sty was just across from the kitchen door, just a little ways away.
So all the animals were right close to the house.
It was just a regular home life between the animals.
You wouldn't bring them in the house, but you had them outside.
PHILLIPS:	So you could, when you were sitting down to eat, for instance, you could look out the kitchen door and there were the pigs.
GRANDE:	Right.
PHILLIPS:	And you had names for the pigs?
GRANDE:	Sometimes.
PHILLIPS:	Did you like the pigs?
GRANDE:	Oh, yeah.
They used to slaughter them in winter and save some meat, cured it, for the summer.
And some of it was for right away.
Not too much fresh, but mostly cured.
PHILLIPS:	How did they cure it?
GRANDE:	Uh, salt and water and pack it, put some rocks on it and 		let it stay like that and then they would smoke it.
PHILLIPS:	Did you used to help do that?
GRANDE:	I had to help with, when they killed the pigs I had to catch the blood.
I didn't like it, but that was part of my job.
PHILLIPS:	Can you perhaps remember actually what you did?
Can you 		describe that for us?
GRANDE:	Well, when my mother got the fellow to kill the pig, well, I had to hold the pan there and catch the blood, because they made the blood sausage out of that.
Of course, they worked with it to make it good, and then they made the blood sausage and usually they fried the liver right away, good and fresh.
That was really good.
Then later, out of the other leftover pieces of regular meat they'd make smoked sausage and they'd smoke them and have them for during the summer.
They had for special occasions, or when they had hired help.
They had different pieces for different times.
PHILLIPS:	So you got a lot of different, uh, meats.
You got your money's worth out of those little pigs across the way from the kitchen.
GRANDE:	Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
And then, of course, we had 		the milk from the cows.
We didn't kill the cows at home.
It's very seldom we had beef but, uh, we chickens, so there was chicken meat and eggs and pigs and milk and cheese, whatever.
(Sound of door opening in the background.)
MITZI STACKHOUSE:  Hello.
GRANDE:	Hi.
PHILLIPS:	Just a small interruption there.
Clyde and Mitzi 		Stackhouse.

Mitzi is the daughter--

	GRANDE:  Yeah.
PHILLIPS:	--of Mrs. Grande.
Okay.
We were back, you were telling 		us a little bit about your life on your farm.
Can you tell us, perhaps, how you warmed the place in the wintertime.
What, was it cold for you?
GRANDE:	Well, we, uh, heated it with the wood.
There was times that my mother later on got some coal, but not very much.
It was mostly wood.
And the forest wasn't far from the house, so we used to get the wood there.
And, of course, she had quite a bit hauled in the fall, so we would have wood.
PHILLIPS:	You say hauled in the fall, she got the wood during the 		fall?
GRANDE:	Yeah, so we'd have it all winter long.
But she would cut it up herself, saw and chop it and, of course, later on when I was bigger I tried to help, which I wasn't much good.
But I helped on the farm what little I could, and I had a mile or better to go to school.
PHILLIPS:	Before you tell us about going to school, can you tell 		me what your life was like for your mother.
She was, just one moment.
We need for you to be as quiet as possible (addressing daughter and son-in-law).
GRANDE:	Well, originally she was a widow and she married her husband.
And then she bought this home.
And then when she met--  (Break in tape.)
PHILLIPS:	Okay, Mrs. Grande, could you continue please?
I think we were just talking about how difficult it was for your mother.
You said she was a widow and married again.
GRANDE:	She was a widow, and she had two boys.
One died before, 		but two of them were living, so she was raising them.
And then I came along after she married with my husband, uh, my father.
And, uh, he came to the States, and there was another sister born after that.
PHILLIPS:	Could you tell me, I mean, is sounds like your mother must have been a tremendously strong and hardy woman to look after a farm and bring up these children and chop the wood.
Was that normal?
GRANDE:	Normal, normal.
Most all the women did similar work, 		worked on the farm and just took care of everything.
There was quite a few men that came to the States to make a livelihood and then probably come back, which a lot of them never did.
A lot of them took their family over here, but a lot of them, some did help.
But during the wartime they couldn't help back and forth at all.
So my mother used to kind of sell milk and eggs and whatever she could, products.
Not too much off the farm because that was our livelihood throughout the year.
And you had to keep some for the seeds for next year.
PHILLIPS:	Did you tell me how ,any were in your family?
Could you 		tell me that?
GRANDE:	Well, there was, actually, uh, four, five, with my mother.
And then my sister died.
Oh, she was about a year old when she died, so there was four of us.
And, uh, we'd done the best we could, just like a lot of others.
PHILLIPS:	Why did your sister die?
What did she die of?
GRANDE:	Childhood problems.
PHILLIPS:	Was that very common in your village?
GRANDE:	Well, there was quite a few children that did die.
I don't know exactly what the cause or what it was, but she died.
PHILLIPS:	Now, could you tell me, please, about going to school.
You said you had to walk to school.
Tell me about your school days.
GRANDE:	Well, it was a good mile, or maybe further, past the cemetery, which I wasn't scared of.
I didn't mind it a bit.
And we crossed the track.
And, of course, one time I didn't pay attention, the weather as bad and I had my head down, the rails were down that the train was going to come,and I bumped in there, which wasn't very good.
But that woke me up to pay attention.
But, uh, we walked back and forth.
We didn't think nothing of it.
That was normal.
Nobody had cars or anything, and they didn't take them with the wagon because there was too much other work to be done.
And then when my brother got older, he was quite a bit older than me, well, uh, he went and stayed with my aunt for a while on a different part of the area.
And so my mother and I were alone for quite a while.
And then she bartered with the cattle, and sometimes I'd stay with the neighbors overnight when she'd go and barter to different places.
And all that she done, all of that walking.
She didn't have no other transportation.
So then after the war, when my dad started writing about coming to the States, naturally we were all enthused about it.
I don't know if my mother was enthused or not, but I was.
PHILLIPS:	Tell me what it was like for you during the war.
Do you 		have any memories of that?
GRANDE:	Well, we had to have refugees from the Italian side, and 		the extra room that we had that was like, it could have been a summer kitchen.
Well, we had to give that to the refugees and everybody had to share their home with the refugees.
And, of course, they spoke quite a bit Italian, but we managed to understand each other pretty good.
PHILLIPS:	Do you know, or do you remember, whether it was the government that forced you to take the refugees, or the people in the community just did it?
GRANDE:	It was more or less a government decision that everybody 		would take some refugees.
And we went down by the river which was about a good half-a-mile or further to wash clothes on the rocks.
I don't know if you ever heard of it or not, but that was, and then my mother would take them sometimes over to the hospital which was the other way.
It wasn't as far.
The only thing, she didn't like to go there because there was too much bloody stuff in there.
PHILLIPS:	Why?
GRANDE:	Uh, to wash clothes in shelter instead of outdoors.
PHILLIPS:	But you said there was some bloody stuff in there.
Why?
GRANDE:	Well.
it was a hospital, and they'd let women come there 		and wash clothes but naturally the hospital supplies were going through first.
PHILLIPS:	So the rubbish and the effluent from the hospital was 		thrown into the river.
GRANDE:	Yeah.
PHILLIPS:	And so you probably tried to wash upstream from the 		hospital.
GRANDE:	Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
Definitely.
And there was two hospitals.
One on one side of the river was for men, and the one on the other side of the river was for women.
They didn't have them all combined together like they have over here now.
They have them like that now too over there.
But at that time there it was each separate.
And there was a castle right across the street from the hospital, which is now tuberculosis sanitarium.
They don't have no castles no more.
So there's quite a bit of changes any more, too.
PHILLIPS:	But in those days who lived in that castle?
GRANDE:	The Austrian people.
They were the higher-ups from the Austrian, not government, but they were well-to-do and all that.

But--

	PHILLIPS:	What language did you speak?
GRANDE:	Actually Slovanian, and we had to take German.
PHILLIPS:	Not Hungarian.
GRANDE:	No, no.
German was our main language, like in office or 		different places, you almost had to learn how to speak German to get along with them.
PHILLIPS:	That was the official language.
GRANDE:	Right.
PHILLIPS:	So how many languages were spoken, then, in your 		village?
GRANDE:	The two.
German and Slovanian.

PHILLIPS:	Do you remember much, at that age, about any political 		structures or political problems, or--

	GRANDE:	All I kind of remember when, I guess it was about 1918 when they was going to overthrow Austria/Hungary altogether and change it to Yugoslavia.
PHILLIPS:	When who was?

GRANDE:	Well, the government was a changing deal because the--

	PHILLIPS:	I'm sorry, which government are you talking about?
GRANDE:	Part of Yugoslavia.
And the First World War started 		down in Sarajevo in what is now Yugoslavia.
It's down more or less in the Serbian section.
That's where Ferdinand got killed and World War One started.
and I kind of remember when they had the meetings around 1918 and '19, '20 and after all that, well, it became Yugoslavia.
So actually I was born in Austria/Hungary and came here from Yugoslavia.
PHILLIPS:	Do you remember what your older relatives, or your mother, or people who came to your house, were talking about during those times?
Were they afraid?
Were they excited?
What was the atmosphere like?
GRANDE:	Well, during the war they were excited and all that but, 		uh, not too much, because you just had to go along.
There were restrictions on certain things you couldn't buy.
And, uh, the people that lived in the city, which was across the river from us, they didn't have food, and we had more food because we were out in the country.
So for any small amount of food, either eggs, or chicken, or no matter what, or milk, they would share clothing because they had plenty of clothes, but they had a hard time getting food.
PHILLIPS:	And so that was, did that become a barter system that 		you, part of the barter system you were describing before, or not?
Or did they pay money for the food?
GRANDE:	Oh, no.
It would be a bartering.
I had, I usually had real nice clothes and, uh, when we were coming here to the States I'd have had beautiful clothes if it wouldn't be stolen in Italy while we were waiting for the ship.
We had to stay there three days and they had us, they had my mother put the suitcases in the storage, and when she went to get them they were gone.
Not only hers, a lot of others too.
PHILLIPS:	Meanwhile, back before that happened, how did the people 		cross that river?

Was there a boat, or a bridge, or--

	GRANDE:	Oh, further down there was a big ridge.
There was, uh, or they went with wagons and whatever needed to be.
Of course, you don't see the wagons now any more.
All you see is cars.
But at that time there was wagons.
PHILLIPS:	When was it that you first learned that you were going 		to travel to America?
GRANDE:	Oh, it was, oh, I guess about, not the very first part, 		but about towards the second part of 1920.
PHILLIPS:	And tell me how you learned about that?
GRANDE:	Well, my mother was telling me about the letters she got 		from my father, and then he sent us the tickets.
And she had to go to different places to get it approved and all that.
But, uh, not too many.
Like some of them had to go through more than she did.
And I didn't have to go anywhere because I was a minor.
So, but on the passport, uh, her and my pictures were together, so everything went, whatever she got, it was for me, too.
PHILLIPS:	And how about the other children?
GRANDE:	Well, my oldest brother, he had to go in the army.
See, 		they have to serve three years all the time.
But, uh, he had to go in.
And then the other one, they was going to bring him here.
And in the spring, when he was supposed to go kind of like to report for the army, he was on the wagon and he fell or jumped or whatever, and he got killed.
So then this one got to come from the army earlier to take over the farm.
PHILLIPS:	Because his brother had been killed.
GRANDE:	Yeah.
PHILLIPS:	And so did your brother accompany you to the United 		States?
GRANDE:	No.
He would have to come after it.
It was just my 		mother and I that came then.
PHILLIPS:	All right.
Now, tell me what you actually had to, to do 		to get your papers.

GRANDE:	Well, I wasn't with my mother too much, but she had to go through quite a bit of different courts and different offices and get approvals of, oh, different higher-ups that were in line with the travelling and all.
So she had to make quite a few different trips.
PHILLIPS:	Do you know how long it took her to get the approval?
GRANDE:	Oh, it was about, the last month before we left it seemed like she was always coming or going to different places.
PHILLIPS:	And meanwhile your father was in America?
GRANDE:	Yeah.
PHILLIPS:	So tell me about finally leaving.
How did you feel?
GRANDE:	Oh, I was anxious just to get here to meet my dad.
PHILLIPS:	Weren't you going to miss your friends?
GRANDE:	I didn't think about it then.
PHILLIPS:	How old were you when you were leaving?
GRANDE: 	Ten.
I was just anxious to meet my dad.
PHILLIPS:	And how had your school life been?

Had you enjoyed 		school back in--

	GRANDE:	Well, I like school.
I had average grades.
I wouldn't say they were exceptional, but I had them average, and I had to take German for two years in third and fourth grade.
Of course, Later on when I came here there was no German people around, so I just kind of forgot it.
But, uh, it would be nice if I had remembered it.
PHILLIPS:	So how did you, you got to Trieste, think you described.
GRANDE:	We went by train.
We went by train, and then we got to 		where we had, where my mother checked in the suitcases and, uh, we just had like a barn place.
It wasn't a hotel or anything, where we stayed for, till 15th.
And then we went on the ship.

And, uh, my dad paid for first class but they put us down on the bottom and then this one fellow spoke up that was, uh, here before, and he seen my mother's card, and he said, "You don't belong down here.
You should go up higher."
And we had mostly fish and macaroni on the ship, which I didn't like very well, but you had to eat something.
PHILLIPS:	So did you manage to get up to first class?
GRANDE:	Not first.
But we got up to second.
We got up to second class.
It was better, but nothing to brag about compared to what the ships are now.
PHILLIPS:	Did you still have to eat fish and macaroni?
GRANDE:	Oh, yes.
I guess that was the basic, it was an Italian ship, so I guess that's what it was.

And they stopped it in London, they stopped it in different places until finally I guess they got a call from the United States where the people are that were supposed to be in by a certain time and they weren't, and so the ship started going real fast, and finally got to New York.
PHILLIPS:	Do you remember that?
GRANDE:	Well, I do some, not too much.
I suppose I was too excited about getting to my dad's place.
I know there was quite a few that had eye disease and they had them there for a few days, and then a lot of them were sent back from where they were coming from, either Italy, Germany or Yugoslavia.

And then uh, well, we were there, and they vaccinated us again and checked us through and all, and finally we got to go and I think we changed trains in Chicago, from New York to Chicago, and then changed trains.
And there were some people that went here to Denver that were from more or less the same area as we were.
But, uh, we got on a different train in Chicago.
The train they got was different than the one we had.
And that's the first time I got to see bananas, on the train.
And, of course, there was a fellow that, he went over there to get his two children, and he didn't buy them very much.
Whatever, the rest of them, like my mother and this other lady bought, we shared with his kids.
They didn't like it very much, but what can you do.
So we all went, there was a lady with her two daughters, and this man with his two children, and my mother and me, we all went to the same town in Utah.
We got off in Price, and we, of course, that's where my dad met us, and then we had to take the bus twenty-eight miles to Sunnyside.
PHILLIPS:	Why was it that you decided to move up to, you were on 		your way to Denver, I take it?
GRANDE:	No, no.
We were on the way to Utah to begin with.
That's where my dad lived.
PHILLIPS:	Why had he chosen to live in Utah?
GRANDE:	Well, he was working in coal mines.
He worked in different mines in Utah, around.
He worked part-time years before that in  Aspen, Colorado, but he went back to Utah around Tooele.
And at that time he was in Sunnyside, and so we lived there about six and a half years, and then his health went bad, and so we came to Colorado.
PHILLIPS:	I see.
Can you tell me how you felt when you arrived in 		the United States and how eventually you must have started school, what was that like for you?
GRANDE:	Well, we lived, my dad rented a company four room house that was a double, and the other family was similar in nationality as we were.
And so, uh, the kids could talk broken Slovanian, so we kind of got started getting along, and I'd pick up words from them little by little.
And we came on a Thursday, and the following Monday I went to school.
And being that these were going to the same grades, so they put me in the fourth grade instead of the first.
And I struggle along pretty good because as far as figures you didn't have to talk.
You could just write, and it was good.
And, uh, so I was in a county contest the first and second year for arithmetic to where we went from Sunnyside to Price, we stayed overnight.
And it was good.
And then I skipped the sixth grade and in seventh grade I was in a running contest.
I wouldn't go now, but I did go then.
PHILLIPS:	Okay.
Let me just turn my tape over.
END OF SIDE ONE

BEGINNING OF SIDE TWO

	PHILLIPS:	This is side two, Interview Number 414 [DP-40], with Mary Grande.
Um, so, uh, you did fairly well at school?
GRANDE:	Yeah, I done, I'd say I wasn't the best, but I was with 		the top ones.
PHILLIPS:	Now, tell me what life was like now for your mother.
She no longer had all of those animals to look after.
It must have been very different.
What did she do?
GRANDE:	All we could do was housework and laundry, cleaning and cooking, and that.
So she got adjusted pretty well.

She always hoped to go back, but she never did because they started talking about the war and she didn't want to be in another war over there, which they had it worse right in our area in the second one than they did in the first.
And my brother, half-brother, of course, he lived there until the '70s when he died.
She gave the property over to him.
And they had pretty rough deals there during the second war.
In fact, one of his daughters got deaf and dumb on account of the noise of the cannons that they were shooting on their farm.
We were right close to where they had the battles and everything, my brother was, so.
But, uh, I got pretty well with the kids in school.
Of course, I couldn't speak, but I started learning very fast the best way I could and I enjoyed the school.
PHILLIPS:	And where were you living at this point?
GRANDE:	In Sunnyside, Utah.
PHILLIPS:	When did you, well, before I ask you that question, uh, can you tell me a little bit about the Depression years for your family.
What was that like?
GRANDE:	Well, the Depression really hit us after I came here to Colorado.
Right after I got married, that's when we had the worst Depression.

But, uh, dad was kind of, his health was going down great, so he couldn't, uh, work and he figured, well, between my mother and him, they figured maybe he could do some outside work over here, and we had, my mother's sister lived up on northeast of Greeley on what they call the dry land, so we were there for a week, and then we came to Denver.
So he got a job finally over at the smelter, and he worked there for a while, but couldn't work too long.
And then little by little he tried to work, he went finally back into the mine, and he couldn't work too long there.
Finally the doctor said he couldn't work no more.
PHILLIPS:	What was, uh, how did you feel, at this point, or how did your family feel about being so far away from home?

Did they miss--

	GRANDE:	My mother missed it a lot.
But, uh, I'm sorry to say, I 		didn't		.
PHILLIPS:	So you got married.
When did you get married?
GRANDE:	In 1927, here in Denver.
And I've been here ever since.
PHILLIPS:	And, uh, you moved to Denver when, from Sunnyside?
GRANDE;	In 1926, in October.
We drove a Model T.  In fact, I drove it most of the way, and we stopped in Aspen.
I had an uncle, my mother's brother, there.
We stopped there for a month, and then we came on to Colorado.
And, uh, after a while we both got jobs, and it worked out.
And I met my husband on New Year's and married him in May.
He was from the same area as I was, and years later we went back there.
He liked the area where I was raised much better than where he was because it was close to the city.
And him and I went back there three times, one by car and twice with the plane.
And, of course, I've been there since a few times.
PHILLIPS:	Why was it that there were so many people from your, 		from that area that you came from in Europe, living in this area around Denver?
Do you know?
GRANDE:	Well, it's a settlement.
When a group gets settled and then somebody else tries to come in, and they just like to integrate together.
PHILLIPS:	Okay.
Is there anything else that you'd like to, think 		we might be interested to hear about?
GRANDE:	Well, I worked later on.
During the Depression it was kind of rough.
My husband wasn't making much.
Worked every other week and the girls were getting to the point, being in school, we sent them to parochial school.
And naturally we had to pay.
It wasn't like now that somebody else pays for them a lot.
And then, uh, U finally got a job in a packing house, which I worked close to thirty years in it.
And in between every so often I had to have different surgeries and I'd be home.
PHILLIPS:	What kind of work, excuse me, what kind of work 		particularly were you doing?
GRANDE:	Cutting up meat, pork and beef.
Cut it for scraps for 		sausage and stuff.

And then later on she came into the packing house and she worked with me for a while, and then she went down in the smokehouse where they have to smoke bacon and ham, and she worked there until she had to give up medically on account of it.
PHILLIPS:	Let me explain for identification who she is.
GRANDE:	My daughter, Mitzi.
PHILLIPS:	M-I-T-Z-I.  Mitzi Stackhouse.
GRANDE:	Yeah.
I have another daughter, Jenny Musk.
She's a 		nurse.
She lives way up in Northglen.
PHILLIPS:	Okay.
Unless there's something else you think you'd 		like to us all.
GRANDE:	Well, we became, my husband and I became American citizens in 1932 and we tried to vote every time ever since.
Maybe not right, but we try our best.
And every time I go back to Europe I'm happy to come back.
This is my country.
PHILLIPS:	Okay.
That finishes Interview Number 414 [DP-40] with 		Mary Grande,  It's five after one.

standard.label: Multi-stage overseas transit routing
standard.definition: Migration journeys constructed as chains of transport modes and waypoints rather than direct travel, requiring coordination across legs.
standard.model_rationale: Cites the train-to-port-then-ship sequence as the analytic concept of staged transit, rather than logging each leg separately.

standard.label: Material vulnerability and dispossession during transit
standard.definition: Encounter with theft and material loss in the course of migration, requiring improvised recovery of basic necessities.
standard.model_rationale: Stolen clothing and the subsequent need for replacement garments mark transit itself as a site of material precarity rather than neutral travel.

standard.label: Diasporic intermediary networks aiding new arrivals
standard.definition: Established migrants or travelers acting as brokers who provide material or logistical support to newcomers during transit.
standard.model_rationale: The man known to the mother who purchases replacement clothing in London exemplifies the recurring pattern in which prior migrants facilitate subsequent waves.

standard.label: Onomastic layering of pre- and post-migration identity
standard.definition: Articulation of the self through maiden name alongside current married name, marking a layered identity spanning place of origin and place of settlement.
standard.model_rationale: The interviewer prompts for maiden name and the speaker supplies a Slavic pre-marriage surname, surfacing the analytic tension between origin identity and current identity common in immigration oral history.

standard.label: Homeland reclassified across successive political regimes
standard.definition: Place of origin framed not as a fixed locale but as a territory whose national and political identity has shifted over time.
standard.model_rationale: The speaker distinguishes the current label (Yugoslavia) from the former one (Austria/Hungary) for the same place, encoding the idea that homeland identity is contingent on regime change.

standard.label: English-language fluency as enabling capital
standard.definition: Command of English ('American') as a practical resource allowing intermediaries to navigate foreign commercial contexts on behalf of non-English-speaking migrants.
standard.model_rationale: The speaker's specific emphasis that the helper 'could speak American' identifies linguistic competence as the mechanism underwriting his capacity to assist.

standard.label: Circular return mobility of established diaspora members
standard.definition: Migrants settled abroad returning temporarily to transit-route cities to assist family or acquaintances still in motion.
standard.model_rationale: The helper 'came back from the States' to London, illustrating the bidirectional circulation characteristic of early diaspora formation rather than unidirectional permanent migration.

standard.label: Childhood amnesia as a structuring feature of early-life migration memory
standard.definition: Gaps and uncertainties in the speaker's recollection that reflect the cognitive limits of migration experienced at a very young age.
standard.model_rationale: The explicit 'I don't remember' about the helper's traveling companions signals that the narrative is reconstructed partly from fragments inherited from adults, not direct childhood memory.

standard.label: Integrated domestic-productive dwelling architecture
standard.definition: The structural unification of human dwelling and animal shelter as a defining feature of peasant household construction.
standard.model_rationale: The three-room home with an attached barn half and adjacent manure pile is repeatedly emphasised as a single building, capturing a built-environment concept rather than a single itinerary item.

standard.label: Transnational family economic strategy
standard.definition: The household's economic base spans borders, with one parent working abroad while the other sustains the rural economy at home.
standard.model_rationale: Father's long residence in the US paired with mother's at-home farming implies a transnational division of family labour, not just biographical fact.

standard.label: Barter-based subsistence economy
standard.definition: Livestock function as a medium of exchange rather than purely market commodities within the household economy.
standard.model_rationale: Grande specifies her mother 'raised some cattle and bartered with them', defining an economic mode rather than an activity.

standard.label: Historical continuity of rural building norms
standard.definition: The combined house-barn form is understood as an inherited tradition whose validity the speaker takes for granted.
standard.model_rationale: Phrases like 'as far as I know', 'I don't know any different', and 'they don't do it now anymore' frame the architecture as inherited customary practice under erosion.

standard.label: Childhood affective bonds with livestock
standard.definition: Children develop emotional attachments to animals kept close to the home, including naming and treating them as pets.
standard.model_rationale: The loved pet cow and named pigs show emotional relations with animals alongside their productive role.

standard.label: Age-graded gendered labour in slaughter and processing
standard.definition: Children, and girls in particular, are assigned specific bodily tasks within the butchering process as part of ordinary domestic duty.
standard.model_rationale: Catching the blood in a pan is presented as 'my job', marking a gendered, age-specific division of slaughter labour.

standard.label: Communal and seasonal slaughter as social event
standard.definition: Pig killing is a wintertime, cooperative occasion drawing on outside labour and producing valued food for participants.
standard.model_rationale: References to winter slaughter, hiring 'the fellow' to kill the pig, and reserving cuts for 'hired help' construct slaughter as a seasonal, cooperative practice.

standard.label: Curing and smoking as preservation regime
standard.definition: Meat is conserved across seasons through salting, pressing, and smoking rather than primarily through fresh consumption.
standard.model_rationale: Salt-and-water packing weighted with rocks and subsequent smoking constitutes a distinct preservation logic aimed at summer provisioning.

standard.label: Triage of animal products by use and occasion
standard.definition: Different cuts and preparations are allocated to immediate consumption, seasonal storage, or special occasions and visitors.
standard.model_rationale: Fresh liver, blood sausage, smoked sausage, and reserved cuts map onto a deliberate differentiation by temporal and social function.

standard.label: Diversified animal-based subsistence
standard.definition: Household food security rests on multiple species yielding complementary products (dairy, eggs, meat, cheese).
standard.model_rationale: The inventory of cows, chickens, pigs and their outputs (milk, cheese, eggs, meat) reflects a portfolio approach to subsistence rather than reliance on a single animal.

standard.label: Normalization of livestock within domestic space
standard.definition: Animals are treated as a routine presence in daily life, sited immediately outside the kitchen and integrated into the household's sensory environment.
standard.model_rationale: Pigs visible from the kitchen door and 'regular home life between the animals' convey an unremarkable embeddedness of livestock in the domestic sphere.

standard.label: Erosion of traditional peasant lifeways
standard.definition: Customary practices around housing, slaughter, and meat preparation are recognised as belonging to a receding past.
standard.model_rationale: Grande marks these routines as historical ('they used to', 'don't do it now anymore'), framing them as a vanishing way of life.

standard.label: Wood-based domestic heating from proximal forest resources
standard.definition: Household warmth is sustained primarily through wood gathered from a nearby forest, with coal used only sparingly and later in time.
standard.model_rationale: Captures the analytic point about the household's energy regime (forest-proximate, wood-dominated) rather than merely recording that wood was burned.

standard.label: Seasonal fall provisioning for winter sustenance
standard.definition: Critical resources are stockpiled in autumn to ensure year-long household survival through winter.
standard.model_rationale: The fall haul is not a literal itinerary step but an analytic pattern of anticipatory seasonal provisioning characteristic of subsistence households.

standard.label: Children's labor contribution to family farm
standard.definition: Children participate in essential farm labor from a young age, supplementing adult work despite limited capacity.
standard.model_rationale: The narrator's self-deprecating account of helping ('wasn't much good') reflects the analytic theme of early childhood integration into household labor needs.

standard.label: Widow remarriage for household reconstitution
standard.definition: A widow remarries and rebuilds a household by purchasing property and producing additional children, forming a reconstituted family.
standard.model_rationale: Beyond chronology of births and marriages, the analytic idea is remarriage as a strategy for household reconstitution after spousal loss.

standard.label: Women's full-spectrum farm competence normalized as ordinary
standard.definition: A woman managing all farm operations alone, including chopping wood and tending fields, is treated as unremarkable because it was widespread among rural women.
standard.model_rationale: Interviewer's framing of mother as exceptionally hardy is gently reframed as normal; the latent point is the gendering of farm labor under male absence.

standard.label: Transatlantic male labor migration producing family separation
standard.definition: Men depart for the United States to seek livelihood, often with no return, leaving women to maintain households and farms.
standard.model_rationale: Beyond an itinerary of departure, the analytic concept is transatlantic labor migration as a structural cause of long-term family separation.

standard.label: Wartime severance of transnational family reciprocity
standard.definition: War disrupts the expected flow of support between migrants abroad and families at home, intensifying women's burden.
standard.model_rationale: The analytic idea is war as a force that interrupts reciprocal transnational family obligations, not merely a chronological marker.

standard.label: Women's microenterprise from farm surplus
standard.definition: Women generate small cash incomes by selling milk, eggs, and other farm products while preserving household subsistence reserves.
standard.model_rationale: Captures the analytic pattern of women converting subsistence surplus into modest market income as a livelihood strategy.

standard.label: Intergenerational seed-saving for agricultural continuity
standard.definition: A portion of harvest is deliberately reserved as seed stock to ensure the next year's crop, embedding long-horizon planning into subsistence farming.
standard.model_rationale: Beyond a gardening detail, this is the analytic concept of seed-saving as a mechanism of agricultural and household continuity across years.

standard.label: Child mortality shaping family composition
standard.definition: Infant and child death is a routine feature of family life that reduces household size and shapes sibling structure.
standard.model_rationale: The mention of a sibling dying at about one year old is not a one-off fact but an analytic indicator of child mortality as a normal shaper of family composition.

standard.label: Collective hardship and shared endurance as moral frame
standard.definition: Personal struggle is located within a broader community of similarly affected families, framing endurance as a shared rather than singular condition.
standard.model_rationale: Phrases like 'just like a lot of others' and 'most all the women did similar work' reveal a latent interpretive frame situating individual biography within collective hardship.

standard.label: Normalization of childhood mortality
standard.definition: The acceptance of child death as a common, unexplained feature of rural village life.
standard.model_rationale: S4.004 explicitly states several children died, and S4.005 expresses resigned ignorance of the cause, signaling a community-level normalization rather than a singular tragedy.

standard.label: Habituation to physical danger in daily routine
standard.definition: Recurring exposure to risk (e.g., rail crossings, cemetery) is incorporated into ordinary life rather than treated as exceptional.
standard.model_rationale: The narrator frames the near-miss with a train as a lesson in attentiveness, not trauma, and explicitly states the walk was 'normal' and unremarkable, illustrating how risk becomes routine.

standard.label: Transport scarcity as a structuring condition of rural life
standard.definition: The absence of cars, wagons, or mechanized travel shaped mobility, labor allocation, and daily rhythms for the whole household.
standard.model_rationale: Walking is repeatedly invoked as the default, with wagon use explicitly precluded by competing farm work, showing that lack of transport organized life rather than being a mere inconvenience.

standard.label: Maternal solitary provisioning through informal exchange
standard.definition: A mother managing household economy alone via bartering livestock and traveling on foot to trade, with no alternative transport.
standard.model_rationale: The mother is depicted as the sole economic agent using cattle bartering and walking as her mode of exchange, characterizing her role as both laborer and breadwinner.

standard.label: Kinship and neighbor networks as childcare infrastructure
standard.definition: Children are redistributed among relatives and neighbors to accommodate adult economic activity and family dispersal.
standard.model_rationale: The brother staying with an aunt and the child sleeping at neighbors' homes are framed as normal arrangements, revealing how the wider social network substituted for institutional childcare.

standard.label: Paternal absence and maternal sole responsibility
standard.definition: The father's emigration trajectory leaves the mother to manage the household and children alone for extended periods.
standard.model_rationale: S4.019 explicitly states mother and child were 'alone for quite a while,' and S4.023 reveals the father was already writing about coming to the States, implying a prolonged separation underpinning the mother's solo labor.

standard.label: Postwar migration optimism and generational divergence
standard.definition: The prospect of transatlantic migration generates enthusiasm in the child while leaving the mother's feelings ambiguous.
standard.model_rationale: The narrator contrasts collective and personal enthusiasm with uncertainty about the mother's stance, marking a generational and gendered split in response to migration.

standard.label: Government-mandated civilian hosting of displaced persons
standard.definition: State directive requiring private households to shelter refugees from combatant zones, reframing domestic space as a requisitioned wartime resource.
standard.model_rationale: Names the policy mechanism (government decision) and its household-level effect (compulsory sharing of the home) rather than the literal presence of guests.

standard.label: Cross-ethnic cohabitation and linguistic accommodation
standard.definition: Hosts and displaced persons managing mutual intelligibility and shared domestic routines despite limited common language.
standard.model_rationale: Captures the relational work of accommodation, not the mere fact of having Italian-speaking guests.

standard.label: Gendered wartime domestic labor at communal sites
standard.definition: Women's laundering labor relocated to public waterways and institutional facilities as part of wartime survival work.
standard.model_rationale: Codes the gendered division and public relocation of survival labor rather than the specific act of washing.

standard.label: Environmental contamination from wartime medical infrastructure
standard.definition: Hospital effluents and contaminated materials discharged into shared waterways, rendering domestic labor sites hazardous and visually distressing.
standard.model_rationale: Names the public-health and ecological consequence of hospital siting on the river, subsuming the 'bloody stuff' detail as a facet of contamination.

standard.label: Spatial adaptation to upstream contamination
standard.definition: Households relocating washing sites upstream of institutional discharge points to avoid polluted water.
standard.model_rationale: Captures the spatial-environmental reasoning rather than the literal relocation.

standard.label: Gender-segregated medical infrastructure
standard.definition: Wartime hospitals organized as physically separate facilities for men and women, often divided by a natural feature such as a river.
standard.model_rationale: Codes the institutional logic of gendered separation across sites rather than the existence of two hospitals.

standard.label: Repurposing of prewar monumental architecture for medical use
standard.definition: Castles and other historic structures converted into wartime or postwar medical facilities in response to demand.
standard.model_rationale: Names the adaptive reuse of monumental buildings, not the mere presence of a sanitarium.

standard.label: Resource triage and institutional priority under scarcity
standard.definition: Under wartime allocation logic, institutional (hospital) supplies were processed before civilian needs, shaping access to shared resources and sites.
standard.model_rationale: Latent framing: the assumption that institutional needs preempt civilian labor even at shared laundering sites.

standard.label: Disappearance of the wartime-era material landscape
standard.definition: Reflective awareness that wartime physical structures and arrangements (castles, sex-segregated hospitals) have been demolished, merged, or replaced in the post-war built environment.
standard.model_rationale: Latent framing of loss and landscape transformation rather than literal description of change.

standard.label: Imperial linguistic hierarchy
standard.definition: German functioned as the language of institutional and economic life while Slovene was the home vernacular, making bilingual acquisition a practical necessity under Austrian rule.
standard.model_rationale: The speaker describes German as the office/public language one 'almost had to learn' to function, with Slovene as the mother tongue, revealing a structural dominance of German in the imperial setting.

standard.label: Childhood memory of geopolitical transition
standard.definition: A child's residual recollection of political meetings and the dismantling of Austria-Hungary into Yugoslavia around 1918-1920.
standard.model_rationale: The speaker twice anchors memory in 1918-1920 and the meetings surrounding political reorganization, marking these as salient childhood impressions of imperial collapse.

standard.label: Retroactive remapping of national origin
standard.definition: Personal origin is re-described through the lens of post-imperial successor states, so that being 'born in Austria/Hungary' and 'coming from Yugoslavia' coexist as layered identity claims.
standard.model_rationale: The phrasing 'born in Austria/Hungary and came here from Yugoslavia' makes visible how a single life trajectory is framed by two successive polities, with the successor state retroactively supplying the emigration country.

standard.label: WWI catalyst as geographic memory
standard.definition: The speaker locates the spark of World War I in the Serbian section of present-day Yugoslavia (Sarajevo, assassination of Ferdinand), tying the war's origin to a specific place.
standard.model_rationale: These sentences situate WWI's outbreak in a precise territorial and ethnic location, demonstrating the speaker's spatialized understanding of the war as connected to the subsequent political restructuring.

standard.label: Resigned endurance of wartime conditions
standard.definition: Wartime atmosphere is described less as fear than as a matter of 'going along' with restrictions and shortages, suggesting pragmatic acceptance rather than dramatic emotion.
standard.model_rationale: The phrases 'excited and all that but, uh, not too much' and 'you just had to go along' convey a posture of compliant endurance, with consumer restrictions normalized as part of wartime life.

standard.label: Urban-rural resource asymmetry
standard.definition: The city across the river lacked food while the countryside had agricultural surplus, producing structurally complementary scarcities between the two settings.
standard.model_rationale: The speaker explicitly contrasts urban food deprivation with rural food abundance, establishing the material preconditions that shape subsequent exchange between the two communities.

standard.label: Barter as informal moral economy
standard.definition: Clothing was exchanged for food rather than purchased, reflecting an informal economy of mutual aid that bridged urban-rural divides under scarcity.
standard.model_rationale: The exchange of food for clothing, plus the speaker's own accumulated nice clothes, frames bartering as a reciprocal practice tied to complementary needs rather than a monetized market transaction.

standard.label: River as rural-urban threshold with obsolete transit
standard.definition: The river separating the village from the city was crossed via bridge and wagons, modes of transit that have since been displaced by automobiles, marking the modernization of the rural-urban link.
standard.model_rationale: The contrast drawn between former wagon crossings and present-day car traffic frames the river as a now-modernized boundary that once mediated rural-urban connection through pre-industrial transport.

standard.label: Paternal sponsorship of family migration
standard.definition: The father already in America initiates the family reunion process by sending letters and travel tickets.
standard.model_rationale: Captures the transatlantic-pioneer pattern where the migrant father drives subsequent family arrivals via correspondence and material arrangements.

standard.label: Maternal administrative labor for emigration
standard.definition: The mother undertakes extensive bureaucratic navigation across courts and offices to secure approvals to travel.
standard.model_rationale: Highlights the gendered, time-intensive paperwork burden borne by the at-home spouse to enable family emigration.

standard.label: Comparative ease of own emigration process
standard.definition: The speaker frames their family's bureaucratic experience as less arduous than that of other families.
standard.model_rationale: The qualifying remark 'not too many' and the comparison to others situates the speaker's process within a community-wide spectrum of difficulty.

standard.label: Derivative legal status of migrating minor
standard.definition: The child's travel documentation is legally subsumed under the mother's, exempting the minor from independent bureaucratic engagement.
standard.model_rationale: The shared passport and 'whatever she got, it was for me, too' reveal how children's migration is procedurally tethered to the mother rather than independently processed.

standard.label: Compulsory military service constraining male emigration
standard.definition: Mandatory army service delays or prevents brothers from migrating, redirecting them to family-farming obligations instead.
standard.model_rationale: Institutional conscription shapes which family members can leave and when, with surviving brother pulled back to manage the farm.

standard.label: Tragic contingency reshaping family migration trajectory
standard.definition: An unforeseen accidental death of a sibling disrupts the planned migration sequence and family role distribution.
standard.model_rationale: Ambiguity about the cause ('fell or jumped or whatever') and the cascading effects on who migrates illustrate how contingency restructures family migration plans.

standard.label: Staged sequential family migration
standard.definition: Family members travel to America in waves rather than as a single unit, with later arrivals anticipated.
standard.model_rationale: Only mother and child travel first; brothers were to follow. This reflects the piecemeal-reunion logic common in chain migration.

standard.label: Reunion longing superseding pre-departure attachments
standard.definition: The child's eagerness to meet the father overrides any anticipated sense of loss regarding friends or place left behind.
standard.model_rationale: The repeated 'anxious just to meet my dad' alongside 'I didn't think about it then' reveals a forward-oriented emotional framing that minimizes the affective cost of leaving.

standard.label: Heritage language attrition following migration
standard.definition: Language skills acquired in the country of origin are lost when the linguistic community is absent in the destination.
standard.model_rationale: Two years of German instruction fades without German-speaking peers, illustrating how migration depletes pre-existing linguistic capital.

standard.label: Makeshift pre-embarkation lodging
standard.definition: Use of informal, non-commercial accommodation (barns, warehouses, or similar) in port cities while awaiting departure.
standard.model_rationale: The 'barn place' that was 'not a hotel' is coded as the concept of transitional migrant lodging near embarkation points.

standard.label: Purchased class status negated by migrant categorization
standard.definition: A family's economic purchase of higher accommodation is overridden by their social designation as migrants, resulting in placement in the lowest class.
standard.model_rationale: The father paid for first class yet the family was put on the bottom; the latent meaning is that migrant identity subordinated purchasing power within the ship's hierarchy.

standard.label: Forced dietary adaptation during transit
standard.definition: Acceptance of unfamiliar or unpalatable food as a necessary condition of travel rather than a matter of preference.
standard.model_rationale: Fish and macaroni eaten despite dislike, normalized as Italian ship fare, captures the concept of obligatory dietary conformity in transit.

standard.label: Voyage schedule disruption resolved by urgency signal
standard.definition: Itineraries of migrant ships were improvised and could be rerouted or accelerated in response to external communications about expected arrivals.
standard.model_rationale: The ship's multiple stops and abrupt acceleration after a call from the US illustrates the ad hoc, communication-dependent logistics of transatlantic migrant shipping.

standard.label: Retrospective temporal reframing of journey hardship
standard.definition: Speakers contextualize past travel conditions by contrasting them with modern standards, distancing the remembered experience from present norms.
standard.model_rationale: The comparison to 'what the ships are now' is a latent analytical move that frames remembered hardship through a generational lens.

standard.label: Immigration health screening and selective admission
standard.definition: Medical inspection and bureaucratic vetting at the port of entry that determined who could enter and who was returned to origin.
standard.model_rationale: The narrative explicitly describes vaccinations, health checks, and the deportation of those with eye disease, capturing the gatekeeping function of arrival processing.

standard.label: Co-migration with regional cohort
standard.definition: Traveling in the company of other families from the same origin region who were bound for the same destination.
standard.model_rationale: Multiple families from the same area journeyed together to Utah, demonstrating chain migration and the clustering of co-nationals en route.

standard.label: Communal reciprocity among migrant travelers
standard.definition: Sharing resources and caring for others' children among traveling migrants as an informal norm of mutual obligation.
standard.model_rationale: The mother and another woman shared purchased food with a fellow traveler's children despite the man's reluctance, reflecting obligation among displaced people.

standard.label: Sensory encounter with American consumer abundance
standard.definition: First exposure to novel consumer goods as a marker of arrival in a new commercial landscape.
standard.model_rationale: Seeing bananas for the first time on the train signals the migrant child's encounter with American consumer novelty.

standard.label: Father's labor migration to extractive industry
standard.definition: Relocation shaped by the geography of mining and other extractive labor markets.
standard.model_rationale: The father's work in coal mines across Utah and previously in Aspen, Colorado, drove the family's destination choice.

standard.label: Health-driven secondary migration
standard.definition: Subsequent relocation triggered by a family member's declining health rather than continued economic opportunity.
standard.model_rationale: After six and a half years in Sunnyside, the family moved to Colorado because of the father's failing health, showing how illness reshapes migration trajectories.

standard.label: Informal peer-mediated language socialization
standard.definition: Acquiring the host-country language through play and interaction with neighboring children rather than formal instruction.
standard.model_rationale: The narrator learned English by picking up words from co-ethnic neighbor children who spoke broken Slovene, illustrating child-led linguistic integration.

standard.label: Accelerated school placement despite limited English
standard.definition: Being assigned to a grade level based on age rather than host-language proficiency or literacy.
standard.model_rationale: Arriving on a Thursday and starting school the following Monday in the fourth grade reflects institutional decisions that prioritized age cohort over language readiness.

standard.label: Non-verbal academic domains as linguistic equalizer
standard.definition: Subject areas such as arithmetic that allow academic achievement independent of host-language proficiency.
standard.model_rationale: The narrator's success in arithmetic contests is explicitly attributed to the fact that figures require no spoken language, revealing a structural advantage of numerical domains for non-native speakers.

standard.label: Domestic role contraction for immigrant women post-migration
standard.definition: After relocation, an immigrant woman's work narrows from outdoor/agricultural labor to indoor household tasks, signaling a contraction of occupational scope.
standard.model_rationale: The interviewer's pivot to the mother's changed life surfaces the contrast between her prior animal-care work and her post-migration domestic-only labor; this is an analytically reusable pattern of gendered occupational deskilling among immigrant women.

standard.label: Forgone return migration under threat of renewed conflict
standard.definition: An immigrant's aspiration to return to the country of origin is abandoned because of perceived danger from an emerging or renewed war.
standard.model_rationale: The mother's lingering hope to return is extinguished by anticipation of a second war; the analytic concept is not 'wanted to go back' but the rationalized deferral of return driven by risk perception.

standard.label: Transnational kin ties preserved through property devolution
standard.definition: Connection to the country of origin is maintained by transferring landed property to relatives who remained there.
standard.model_rationale: Ceding the property to the half-brother in Italy functions as a transnational family-formation strategy, not merely a bequest; it institutionalizes ongoing ties to the place of origin.

standard.label: Asymmetric war vulnerability between diaspora and origin
standard.definition: Relatives who remained in the sending country bear direct, bodily exposure to combat, while the diaspora experiences war only indirectly.
standard.model_rationale: The niece's deafness from cannon fire and the explicit comparison that the second war was 'worse right in our area' than for the speaker in Utah articulate a structural asymmetry of war risk across the migration divide.

standard.label: Rapid childhood linguistic and social assimilation
standard.definition: Children of immigrants acquire the host-country language quickly and become socially integrated in school despite an initial non-speaker status.
standard.model_rationale: The speaker's self-report of moving from inability to speak to enjoying school illustrates a recurring generational pattern: children acculturate faster than their parents.

standard.label: Kin-mediated chain migration to a new economic frontier
standard.definition: Relocation to a new region is routed through and dependent on the prior presence of a relative in the destination.
standard.model_rationale: The week-long stay with the maternal aunt near Greeley before moving to Denver shows that the Colorado migration was not autonomous but staged through an existing kin node — a classic chain-migration logic.

standard.label: Health-driven exit from the breadwinner role
standard.definition: A male head of household is forced out of wage labor by deteriorating health, as confirmed by medical authority.
standard.model_rationale: The father's progressive inability to work, culminating in the doctor's prohibition, illustrates a common working-class trajectory in which bodily decline terminates economic productivity.

standard.label: Sequential precarious industrial labor under declining health
standard.definition: An ailing worker cycles through a series of short-term, physically demanding industrial jobs without securing stable employment.
standard.model_rationale: The smelter and mine stints are not itinerary details but a pattern of precarious, health-compromising work typical of Depression-era labor markets; coding the concept rather than each job.

standard.label: Deferred onset of macroeconomic crisis in a migrant household
standard.definition: The hardship of a national economic crisis reaches a migrant family not in their first settlement but only after a subsequent relocation and life transition.
standard.model_rationale: The interviewer's framing and the speaker's answer together establish that the Depression's worst effects were temporally and spatially displaced from the original migration, hitting hardest in Colorado after marriage.

standard.label: Differential nostalgia within immigrant household
standard.definition: Within the same family, members diverge in their attachment to or longing for the homeland, with the speaker disclaiming the nostalgia felt by a parent.
standard.model_rationale: Captures the analytic point that homesickness is unevenly distributed across generations or individuals, not a uniform migrant condition.

standard.label: Kinship-staged migration transit
standard.definition: Relocation unfolds as a chain of stays with kin en route, with family members serving as nodes in a multi-leg journey.
standard.model_rationale: Codes the concept of kin-mediated, staggered movement rather than logging each travel leg as its own item.

standard.label: Ethnic clustering as settlement logic
standard.definition: Immigrants cluster in particular places because early arrivals establish a community that absorbs subsequent newcomers, producing integrated co-ethnic enclaves.
standard.model_rationale: Names the underlying social mechanism the speaker invokes to explain immigrant geographic concentration, not just the fact of clustering.

standard.label: Rapid intra-community courtship and marriage
standard.definition: Marriage occurs quickly and within the co-ethnic pool, with spouses drawn from a shared regional origin.
standard.model_rationale: Captures how endogamous community networks compress the courtship-to-marriage timeline.

standard.label: Selective return migration and homeland revaluation
standard.definition: Return trips to the homeland occur selectively over the life course, and spouses reassess the origin place favorably, especially against proximity to a city.
standard.model_rationale: Codes the meaning of repeated return visits and the comparative reframing of the homeland rather than each trip as an itinerary event.

standard.label: Depression-era household precarity and gendered coping
standard.definition: Economic scarcity during the Depression requires both spouses to work, intermittent male employment, and private sacrifice to fund children's schooling.
standard.model_rationale: Names the household-level analytic pattern of distress and the gendered labor response, not just the fact of hard times.

standard.label: Long-tenured industrial labor as economic mainstay
standard.definition: Decades of stable employment in a single industrial workplace function as the household's economic anchor, even when interrupted by health events.
standard.model_rationale: Codes the concept of industrial tenure as a structuring economic resource across the adult life course.

standard.label: Intergenerational occupational transmission in meatpacking
standard.definition: A daughter enters the same industrial workplace as the mother and follows a gendered occupational pathway within the plant until health forces exit.
standard.model_rationale: Captures how industrial employment is reproduced across generations within a family, including lateral moves between plant departments.

standard.label: Citizenship as enacted belonging through civic ritual
standard.definition: Naturalization is framed not as a legal status but as an ongoing moral practice—voting—coupled with an explicit identity claim of the country as one's own.
standard.model_rationale: Reads the latent meaning beneath naturalization-as-fact: citizenship is performed through voting and affirmed through an emotional claim of ownership.

standard.label: Daughters' divergent geographic and occupational trajectories
standard.definition: Female children disperse into different professions and residential locations, signaling upward or lateral mobility away from the parental industrial setting.
standard.model_rationale: Codes the analytic pattern of differentiated second-generation outcomes rather than enumerating each daughter as a separate fact.

standard.label: Formal archival closure of oral history interview
standard.definition: The interviewer's procedural sign-off identifying the interview number, project code, interviewee name, and timestamp, reflecting standardized oral-history archival conventions.
standard.model_rationale: The closing turn assigns the recording an interview ID (414/DP-40), names the interviewee, and records the clock time, illustrating the standard bookend practice that orients later archival use of the recording.

critical.label: Border reorganization as structural driver of migration
critical.definition: Shifts in state sovereignty and political geography (here, the dissolution of Austria-Hungary and formation of Yugoslavia) are identified as the macro-political force producing this family's mobility, rather than purely individual choice.
critical.model_rationale: The narrator flags the change of regime (Austria/Hungary to 'Yugoslavia now') at the moment of stating her origin; state formation/redrawing of borders is the structural backdrop she implicitly names but does not theorize.

critical.label: Post-war macro-structural conditions enabling movement
critical.definition: The aftermath of World War I is presented as the structuring context that shapes the route, pace, and infrastructure of the journey, including why the ship stopped at multiple ports.
critical.model_rationale: The narrator explicitly anchors the itinerary to 'right after World War One' and treats the multi-stop Atlantic passage as a consequence of that structural moment — war as a producer of mobility and reconfigured transport flows.

critical.label: Family-household as the migrating unit
critical.definition: The mother-and-child dyad, not the individual, is the unit that moves; the household absorbs and organizes the dislocation, consistent with Bodnar's framing of the family as mediator of capitalist/structural displacement.
critical.model_rationale: 'My mother and I' is the repeated grammatical subject of departure; migration is narrated as a household event, not a lone decision.

critical.label: Kin-based brokerage and linguistic capital in transit
critical.definition: A male acquaintance of the mother intervenes as a broker in a foreign port because he possesses English/American linguistic and cultural capital, illustrating how pre-existing social networks — and the uneven distribution of language ability — materially shape migrants' passage.
critical.model_rationale: The narrator names 'he could speak American' as the salient qualification of the man who replaced the stolen clothes; English capacity functions as a portable resource enabling action in the migration field, mobilized through a kin-like tie.

critical.label: Material precarity of migrants in transit zones
critical.definition: Theft of clothing in Trieste marks the migrant's unprotected, disposable status in transit, where belongings can be lost without recourse and recovery depends on personal networks rather than state protection.
critical.model_rationale: The casual narration of theft, followed by a network-based workaround rather than any appeal to authority, signals the structural vulnerability of people-in-motion and the absence of institutional safeguards for them.

critical.label: Transnationally divided peasant household with gendered labor split
critical.definition: A single peasant family straddles two economies: the father is absent in the US wage-labor market while the mother sustains the home through farming and barter, allocating household reproduction and wage-earning across national borders and gender lines.
critical.model_rationale: These two sentences establish the material fact of the split household: father in US since 1911, mother as farmer engaged in non-wage exchange. The lens reads this as the structural precondition for migration, not merely a family arrangement.

critical.label: Integrated domestic-productive peasant dwelling
critical.definition: The peasant home fuses domestic dwelling and productive site into a single building: the barn for cattle and horses is structurally part of the house, dissolving any separation between home life and agricultural work.
critical.model_rationale: These sentences describe the spatial-material fact of the barn-as-part-of-house. The lens reads this as the household functioning as a unit of production, not merely consumption, a key feature of the family-household mediating capitalist relations.

critical.label: Subsistence production and home-based food processing
critical.definition: The household slaughters, cures, smokes, and stores its own meat and raises multiple species (cattle, pigs, chickens) for diversified home consumption rather than market sale, performing the work of social reproduction on-site.
critical.model_rationale: The narrator documents an entire chain of home-based food production: slaughter, blood sausage, fresh liver, smoked sausage, curing with salt and weights, plus milk, eggs, cheese. The lens reads this as subsistence production that externalizes the cost of feeding the family away from the wage economy.

critical.label: Non-market and barter exchange relation
critical.definition: The mother's economic activity is conducted through barter of cattle rather than wage labor or market sale, situating the peasant household partially outside monetary circuits.
critical.model_rationale: The narrator's explicit mention of bartering is the only direct evidence in the section, but it is enough to mark the household as operating through non-wage exchange, contrasting with the father's insertion into the US wage labor market.

critical.label: Child labor integrated into household production
critical.definition: Even young children perform necessary tasks in the household's productive cycle (catching blood during slaughter), framed by the narrator as 'my job'; the family operates as a labor pool, not only a residence.
critical.model_rationale: The narrator's own framing of the work as 'my job' identifies the child's participation as labor within a family-based productive unit. The lens reads this as evidence of the family-household as a unit of both production and reproduction.

critical.label: Externalization of social reproduction across borders
critical.definition: The US economy receives the father's labor power while the home region, through the mother's farming, barter, and on-site food production, bears the costs of feeding, housing, and raising the rest of the family; social reproduction is performed largely outside the wage relation.
critical.model_rationale: The structural relation implied across the section: the father's wage labor abroad is sustained by a materially self-provisioning home. This is the externalization frame: the sending society pays to produce and sustain the worker, while the receiving economy collects his labor.

critical.label: Erosion of the integrated peasant household form
critical.definition: The narrator's observation that 'they don't do it now any more like that' signals the historical disruption of the barn-house peasant dwelling, locating the migrant family within a transforming rural economy.
critical.model_rationale: The narrator marks historical change. The lens reads this as the broader transformation of peasant economy, the structural context that pushes migration by making the old household form unviable or past.

critical.label: Affective embedding of the child in peasant productive life
critical.definition: The narrator records attachment, enjoyment, and pride in the home animals and food (a loved pet cow, enjoying the pigs' presence, 'that was really good' about fresh liver); the peasant home is experienced as a site of meaning, not solely as a labor input, and this experienced life resists reduction to economic function.
critical.model_rationale: The rule requires recording the narrator's own agency and pride as data alongside structural points. These sentences show the peasant home is loved, not merely endured, naming the tension between the structural reading and the lived attachment so the lens does not overwrite the experienced with the structural.

critical.label: Sending household as cost-bearer of migrant social reproduction
critical.definition: When a worker migrates for livelihood, the home household absorbs the unpaid labor of maintaining the farm, raising children, and producing subsistence, externalizing the cost of social reproduction to the sending society.
critical.model_rationale: The mother maintains the farm alone, raises the children, sells milk and eggs, and chops wood while the father is in the United States; the home society bears the costs the host economy does not.

critical.label: Capital-induced labor mobility across the Atlantic
critical.definition: Movement to the United States is framed by the narrator as driven by the need to "make a livelihood," reflecting economic-structural compulsion to migrate rather than free choice.
critical.model_rationale: "Quite a few men that came to the States to make a livelihood" locates the cause of migration in economic pressure producing cross-border labor mobility, with men unsure whether they will return.

critical.label: Wartime severance of the transatlantic family economy
critical.definition: War functions as a structural shock that cuts off the back-and-forth flow of remittances, visits, and return migration on which the split-household migrant family depends.
critical.model_rationale: "During the wartime they couldn't help back and forth at all" directly identifies war as severing the material ties that sustained the mother and children in the sending country.

critical.label: Gendered substitution of female labor for the absent migrant
critical.definition: Women absorb the full range of farm labor, household maintenance, and child-rearing work that would otherwise be shared, when male partners migrate.
critical.model_rationale: "Most all the women did similar work, worked on the farm and just took care of everything" generalizes the mother's experience into a structural pattern of female labor substituting for absent male migrants.

critical.label: Family-household as mediator of capitalist dislocation
critical.definition: The split household, with men abroad and women and children in the sending country, sometimes reunifying later, is the institutional form through which the family absorbs and mediates the dislocations of labor mobility.
critical.model_rationale: Widowhood, remarriage, a child born abroad, and a sister born at home describe a household reorganized around migration; the family rather than the individual or the market absorbs the dislocation.

critical.label: Subsistence-plus-petty-sales as sending-economy mode
critical.definition: The sending household reproduces itself through a mix of subsistence production and small-scale market sales of milk, eggs, and farm products, while retaining seed and stock for the next cycle — a survival economy with strict limits on extraction.
critical.model_rationale: "Sell milk and eggs... Not too much off the farm because that was our livelihood throughout the year... keep some for the seeds for next year" names the material mode of household reproduction on the sending side.

critical.label: Collective recognition of shared precarity
critical.definition: The narrator situates the family's struggle within a wider structural commonality, framing individual hardship as one instance of a shared condition faced by "a lot of others."
critical.model_rationale: "Most all the women did similar work... we'd done the best we could, just like a lot of others" records the narrator's own framing of her and her mother as part of a structurally common experience, preserving their agency and dignity as data.

critical.label: Rural transport poverty as material infrastructure deficit
critical.definition: The absence of vehicles and reliance on walking for school, work, and exchange, marking a structural shortfall of infrastructure in the home society.
critical.model_rationale: Repeated references to walking distances, the lack of cars, and the absence of any transportation for the mother indicate a material deprivation that organizes everyday life, not a personal inconvenience.

critical.label: Productive labor prioritized over dependents' mobility
critical.definition: Household means of transport (the wagon) are reserved for farm work rather than ferrying children to school, subordinating education and dependents' needs to subsistence production.
critical.model_rationale: The narrator names the reason children are not driven: the wagon is needed because there is 'too much other work to be done,' revealing a structural ordering in which labor demands override schooling logistics.

critical.label: Barter and extra-monetary household economy
critical.definition: Household provisioning conducted outside the wage labor market through exchange of livestock and goods, signaling a subsistence or semi-subsistence economic base.
