phenomenological.definition: The narrator's first orientation in the new city is structured by the spatial distribution of relatives rather than by streets, landmarks, or institutions.
phenomenological.model_rationale: Rodwin's initial geography of New York is 'One was on Forty second Street. One was in the Bronx. They were all over' — a kin-centered mapping consistent with Halbwachs's social frameworks of memory, where familial belonging structures spatial cognition before any civic orientation.

phenomenological.label: Kinesthetic-occupational first impressions of the neighborhood
phenomenological.definition: The earliest remembered images of the new place are sensory-motor scenes bound to the family's daily work rather than architectural, locational, or scenic features.
phenomenological.model_rationale: When asked for first impressions of the Lower East Side, Rodwin recalls horses, wagons, and bakery deliveries — the moving elements of the family trade, not buildings or street layout — showing how the natural attitude of a working child organizes the lifeworld around the family's livelihood.

phenomenological.label: Assertion of memory against the interviewer's framing
phenomenological.definition: The narrator insists on remembering what the interviewer has suggested she would not, contesting an external assumption about the limits of early-childhood memory.
phenomenological.model_rationale: Levine assumes 'you probably didn't even remember him'; Rodwin counters 'Oh, I did,' asserting her own mnemonic authority on a childhood encounter — a small moment of the narrator claiming the interiority of her memory against an outsider's presumption.

phenomenological.label: Family occupational history as tangled, co-constructed reconstruction
phenomenological.definition: The story of parents' trades emerges through hesitation, self-correction, and word-search, indexing that the occupational genealogy is a received family narrative worked out in the telling rather than directly recalled.
phenomenological.model_rationale: Rodwin works through who was baker and who was tailor in real time, stumbling over 'what do you call it?' and reversing herself — the search-for-the-word and the self-correction are themselves evidence that this is a constructed family narrative, with Portelli-style errors-in-the-telling pointing to its subjective transmission.

phenomenological.label: Affective silence around the paternal reunion
phenomenological.definition: The felt experience of reunion with the long-absent father is conspicuously absent from the account; the linguistic form of address is offered in place of any emotional description.
phenomenological.model_rationale: Neither interviewer nor narrator addresses how it felt to encounter the father again after years of separation; the only substantive content volunteered about reunion is the Daddy/Papa naming distinction. Per Passerini, the silence is eloquent — what is held back from both question and answer carries meaning about what could not or would not be said.

phenomenological.label: Tenement lifeworld as unremarkable background
phenomenological.definition: The material conditions of the Essex Street apartment (shared hall bathroom, coin-operated gas meters) are recalled only in response to structured interviewer prompts, with minimal elaboration, indexing these conditions as the taken-for-granted natural attitude rather than salient memory.
phenomenological.model_rationale: The narrator's contributions here are nearly all one-word confirmations ('Yeah,' 'Three—three—three—') that only surface when the interviewer supplies the specific category; the material environment registers as background rather than as something to dwell on.

phenomenological.label: Bakery as sensory and affective anchor of memory
phenomenological.definition: The family bakery across the street is recalled with sensory specificity (almonds, cherries, macaroons, holiday work) and positive affect, marking it as the site where the narrator's memory lingers and constructs meaning.
phenomenological.model_rationale: Where the tenement prompts monosyllables, the bakery elicits concrete sensory detail and a positive evaluation; the contrast itself reveals where the narrator's memory is invested.

phenomenological.label: Memory reconstructing itself in dialogue
phenomenological.definition: The narrator's account of schooling shifts mid-exchange — first the brothers 'didn't go,' then the school 'didn't take them,' then 'we all went' — as recollection is rebuilt through the back-and-forth of the interview rather than retrieved as a fixed record.
phenomenological.model_rationale: The contradiction and self-correction are not errors to be fact-checked but data about how the narrator reconstructs the family's school history in the present, consistent with Portelli's 'different credibility' of memory.

phenomenological.label: Institutional framing of the family as a labor unit
phenomenological.definition: The school's directive — 'Let them stay home and help you' — reveals how the surrounding social framework positioned the immigrant family primarily as a working unit, with formal schooling secondary to household economic function.
phenomenological.model_rationale: The narrator reports an institutional response that frames children's labor as the expected contribution; this is a social framework of memory (Halbwachs) inscribed in the family's recollection of schooling.

phenomenological.label: Embodied interruption breaking the interview frame
phenomenological.definition: When asked to describe the Lower East Side, the narrator responds with a bodily need ('I have to eat'), a moment where the lifeworld of embodied existence asserts itself over the discursive frame and the larger question goes unanswered.
phenomenological.model_rationale: Read through Passerini's eloquent silence, the body's intrusion is itself a datum: the open-ended question about the neighborhood is met not with narrative but with an embodied claim, and what is not said becomes the point.

phenomenological.label: Prior knowledge as self-positioning
phenomenological.definition: The narrator's claim that he 'knew a lot of things before you started' positions the self as already-formed, carrying pre-migration (London) experience that preceded and exceeded American schooling.
phenomenological.model_rationale: Starting school at three in London is recalled as a source of prior competence; the telling constructs a self who arrived already educated, framing the American school not as origin but as a continuation.

phenomenological.label: Word-finding hesitation as mnemonic texture
phenomenological.definition: Repeated loss of specific nouns mid-utterance, surfacing the past as fragmentary retrieval rather than fluent narrative.
phenomenological.model_rationale: She gropes for the word 'delivery truck' and begins her husband's name with what sounds like 'Mor—' before reaching 'Jack,' demonstrating how concrete referents for past persons and objects are no longer readily available, and that hesitation itself is part of how memory presents.

phenomenological.label: Family cohesion as remembered affective ground
phenomenological.definition: Togetherness is singled out and doubled for emphasis as the felt character of the family years.
phenomenological.model_rationale: 'Always together' is repeated in successive turns, marking cohesion as the salient affective takeaway rather than any single event; the assessment then opens onto a positive descriptor that is never completed.

phenomenological.label: Bakery as structuring occupational world
phenomenological.definition: The bakery recurs across generations and across locations as the material and social setting of family life.
phenomenological.model_rationale: Father's bakery on Essex Street, the husband's bakery work, and the family's later bakery in Rockaway Beach together form the occupational scaffold within which the family is remembered.

phenomenological.label: Reconstructive time-anchoring through self-age
phenomenological.definition: Past dates are estimated by working backward from the narrator's childhood age rather than recalled as calendar time.
phenomenological.model_rationale: 'I must have been about four or five' and 'about fifteen years' show autobiographical memory anchored to self-age and felt duration, the kind of reconstruction Portelli treats as data about subjectivity rather than as fact-checking material.

phenomenological.label: The unfinished assessment as eloquent limit
phenomenological.definition: A positive evaluation of the past is begun and abandoned mid-phrase, leaving the evaluative word unspoken.
phenomenological.model_rationale: 'it—it was a nice—' trails off without a completing adjective. The silence is eloquent in Passerini's sense: the inarticulacy may register that ordinary contentment resists the available praise vocabulary, or that the present-day teller can no longer summon a word adequate to what was then simply lived.

phenomenological.label: Homeworld as backgrounded long residence
phenomenological.definition: An extended period of everyday dwelling is reported flatly, with the subsequent move receiving comparatively more narrative weight.
phenomenological.model_rationale: Fifteen years on the East Side is delivered in one line; the move to Rockaway Beach, by contrast, is the event the telling pauses on. The long residence exemplifies Schutz's lifeworld — taken for granted, lived before reflection — and the teller does not work to recover it.

phenomenological.label: Meeting embedded in a shared occupational milieu
phenomenological.definition: The encounter with the future husband occurs within the same trade world (bakery work) that organized the narrator's own family life.
phenomenological.model_rationale: The husband drove a delivery truck for a bakery and the narrator worked in her family's bakery; courtship is thus framed by a Halbwachs-style social framework in which the trade is the medium of acquaintance, not a separable biographical fact.

phenomenological.label: Domestic and workplace site as kin-gathering anchor
phenomenological.definition: The father's bakery and the family home function as the spatial-social framework through which immigrant kinship is recollected, with relatives' Friday visits and a household 'always' open to kin.
phenomenological.model_rationale: Memory of the social world is organized around concrete places — the bakery on Fridays, the house where relatives gathered — exemplifying Halbwachs-style recollection structured by family setting.

phenomenological.label: Memory gap at the arrival site (Ellis Island)
phenomenological.definition: A tentative recollection of having been at Ellis Island that collapses into 'Can't remember,' marking a limit of autobiographical memory consistent with arrival in early childhood and inviting attention as eloquent silence rather than as failed recall.
phenomenological.model_rationale: The brief flicker of possible memory followed by explicit forgetting is the section's most pointed mnemonic moment; the gap is treated here as a site for attention — what an arriving toddler could not encode — rather than as a deficit.

phenomenological.label: Conjugal bond as precondition for migration
phenomenological.definition: The mother's willingness to emigrate is framed entirely through her tie to the father, with the marriage presented as the unquestioned organizing principle of her lifeworld rather than as one factor among many.
phenomenological.model_rationale: Rodwin renders the mother's orientation in the natural attitude: her decision follows her husband's as a matter of course, and the interviewer simply echoes the formulation — typification at work in the telling.

phenomenological.label: Descendants as present-time anchor of meaning
phenomenological.definition: A late-interview pivot to children and grandchildren, accompanied by the impulse to display photographs, indexing family continuity as the present-day meaning of the immigration story.
phenomenological.model_rationale: When asked how things are 'now,' the narrator turns from evaluation to enumeration of descendants and offers visual proof; what the life has come to mean is located in the next generations rather than in retrospection on the crossing itself.

dp-40-grande-m.central_concept: In Mary Grande's account, the household absorbs the unpaid costs of social reproduction across the migration arc.

subthemes.claim: The pre-migration peasant household was transnationally divided, with the father in the US wage economy from 1911 and the mother sustaining the rural farm, child-rearing, and bureaucratic emigration labor alone.

In the US settlement, the mother's labor narrowed from outdoor agricultural work to indoor domestic tasks, while the family entered segmented industrial workplaces (mining, smelting, packing house) whose bodily costs were privately absorbed.

dp-40-grande-m.falsified_if: The narrative presented the household's costs as equitably distributed across the receiving economy and the homeland, or located primary cost-bearing outside the family unit.

dp-40-grande-m.central_concept: In Mary Grande's account, macro-structural forces author the migration trajectory.

subthemes.claim: These forces include the dissolution of Austria-Hungary into Yugoslavia, post-WWI shipping conditions, US extractive-industry labor demand, state bureaucratic gatekeeping, medical inspection at entry, compulsory military service, the Great Depression, and renewed European war.

The narrative registers these forces obliquely through a personal-emotional register that locates causation in private feeling — the child's longing to meet her father, the mother's homesickness, the adult speaker's claim of belonging — rather than naming the structural forces directly.

dp-40-grande-m.falsified_if: The narrative consistently named the macro-structural forces as the primary drivers of specific decisions, with no gap between structural causation and personal-emotional narration.

dp-40-grande-m.central_concept: In Mary Grande's account, migrant transit is a site of structural exposure.

subthemes.claim: Migrant bodies are unprotected from dispossession across the multi-stage journey: theft of belongings at port-city lodgings, downgrade of paid passage to a lower class, makeshift pre-embarkation accommodation, dietary minimalism on board, and voyage rescheduling under labor-demand pressure.

Recovery from this exposure depends on prior-migrant brokers — fellow travelers, returning diaspora members, strangers aboard ship — whose English-language and procedural capital enable navigation of foreign commerce and shipping hierarchy on the newcomer's behalf.

dp-40-grande-m.falsified_if: The transit narrative showed institutional protection, migrant preparedness, or independent recovery from dispossession that did not depend on prior-migrant brokers.

dp-40-grande-m.central_concept: In Mary Grande's account, the homeland is constituted in memory as a coherent, integrated lifeworld.

subthemes.claim: The lifeworld comprised combined human-animal dwelling, on-site slaughter and cure, multi-species subsistence, barter across the urban-rural divide, gendered division of farm labor, and affective bonds between children and livestock — a single coherent order of daily life.

Its features are explicitly marked as vanished through present-tense comparisons with American modernity — wagons displaced by cars, castles repurposed as sanitariums, combined barn-house dwellings discontinued, sex-segregated hospitals merged.

dp-40-grande-m.falsified_if: The homeland was consistently recalled as continuous with the present, or the narrator's comparisons with American modernity were absent or trivial.

dp-40-grande-m.central_concept: In Mary Grande's account, structural hardship — long walks to school, child mortality, train-track near-misses, gendered labor absorption — is recounted as unremarkable and ordinary rather than affectively dramatic, reproducing the natural attitude in which deprivation is the taken-for-granted horizon of daily life.
dp-40-grande-m.falsified_if: The narrator's recollection of hardship was affectively dramatic, evaluative, or framed as exceptional rather than matter-of-fact.

ei-845-rodwin.central_concept: In the narrators' accounts, the household absorbs the unpaid costs of social reproduction across the migration arc.

subthemes.claim: The migrating household is transnationally divided and reconstituted in stages, with kin in the sending society absorbing the reproduction costs of family members who cannot migrate.

In the receiving society, the household operates as the unit of labor mobilization, with children's formal schooling subordinated to the family enterprise and the bodily costs of work privately absorbed.
The kin network in the receiving society functions as the primary reception and support infrastructure, with the family home and workplace operating as the standing site of kin gathering.

ei-845-rodwin.falsified_if: The narrators' accounts show that institutional or state support, rather than the household itself, bears the costs of social reproduction across the migration arc.

ei-845-rodwin.central_concept: In the narrators' accounts, macro-structural forces author the migration trajectory.

subthemes.claim: These forces include state conscription, the dissolution and shifting of national borders, war and geopolitical upheaval, state bureaucratic gatekeeping, US labor demand, and economic crisis.

The narrative registers these forces obliquely through personal-emotional framings — in-law conflict, conjugal loyalty, longing, homesickness, belonging — that locate causation in private feeling rather than naming structural forces directly.

ei-845-rodwin.falsified_if: The narrators' accounts show migration driven by named individual choice without state, war, or labor-market forces, and without personal-emotional framing of structural causes.

ei-845-rodwin.central_concept: In the narrators' accounts, migrant transit is a site of structural segmentation in which class, ethnicity, and religion organize the migrant's experience.

subthemes.claim: Migrant bodies are subject to class stratification (steerage, money-throwing economy) and ethnic-religious segmentation (designated shipboard sections, kosher provision) within the transit infrastructure.

Community infrastructure — prior-migrant brokers, co-ethnic shipboard staff, and stranger-care networks — partially offsets exposure, sustaining the migrant's religious-cultural life and providing emergency care for vulnerable bodies.

ei-845-rodwin.falsified_if: The narrators' accounts show transit as an unstructured or purely individual experience without class, ethnic, or religious organization.

ei-845-rodwin.central_concept: In Mary Grande's account, the homeland is constituted in memory as a coherent, integrated lifeworld.

subthemes.claim: The lifeworld comprised combined human-animal dwelling, on-site slaughter and cure, multi-species subsistence, barter across the urban-rural divide, gendered division of farm labor, and affective bonds between children and livestock — a single coherent order of daily life.

Its features are explicitly marked as vanished through present-tense comparisons with American modernity — wagons displaced by cars, castles repurposed as sanitariums, combined barn-house dwellings discontinued, sex-segregated hospitals merged.

ei-845-rodwin.falsified_if: Mary Grande's account shows the homeland as fragmented, inaccessible, or recalled only through inherited transmission rather than as a coherent remembered lifeworld.

ei-845-rodwin.central_concept: In the narrators' accounts, structural hardship is recounted as unremarkable and ordinary rather than affectively dramatic, reproducing the natural attitude in which deprivation is the taken-for-granted horizon of daily life.

subthemes.claim: The recounting deploys stock phrases, low-intensity positive evaluations, memory gaps, and dysfluencies as typifications that flatten affect into familiar categories and contain structural ruptures within the routine.

ei-845-rodwin.falsified_if: The narrators' accounts show strong emotional elaboration of hardship, or if structural conditions are narrated as exceptional ruptures rather than as the taken-for-granted horizon of daily life.

ei-845-rodwin.central_concept: In Minnie Rodwin's account, the homeland is inaccessible to direct memory and is constituted through inherited transmission and fragmentary sensory scene-fragments at the autobiography threshold.

subthemes.claim: Pre-memorial self-knowledge is socially transmitted through the mother's voice, family photographs, official documents, and inherited narrative rather than direct recollection.
Earliest memories take the form of halting sensory scene-fragments — a neighbor's call, a household object, a stranger's care — organized around immediate encounter rather than continuous narrative.

ei-845-rodwin.falsified_if: Minnie Rodwin's account shows continuous, direct autobiographical memory of England and Poland, or if the homeland is constituted primarily through her own sensory experience rather than through inherited transmission.

ei-845-rodwin.central_concept: In Minnie Rodwin's account, the family ethnic enterprise operates as the spatial-social anchor of immigrant settlement, fusing commerce, kinship, labor mobilization, and intra-ethnic marriage into a single institutional form.

subthemes.claim: The transferable artisanal trade (the bakery) crosses the migration intact, providing the material continuity between the sending and receiving societies and enabling a gendered occupational transformation within the household.
The enterprise doubles as kin social hub and intra-ethnic marriage market, with the family name and the workplace-residence site organizing the social world of the immigrant community.

ei-845-rodwin.falsified_if: Minnie Rodwin's account shows the family enterprise as marginal or failing, or shows the kin network and marriage market organized independently of the enterprise.
